description: Convention: cursor-based pagination is mandatory for all paged GraphQL endpoints

conversation.title: GraphQL pagination strategy for the public API

turns.text: We have three new paged endpoints to ship next sprint. Some teams want offset/limit because it's simpler. What should we standardise on?

Offset/limit is fine for stable, append-only data, but breaks the moment items can be inserted, edited, or deleted between page fetches — you either skip rows or repeat them. For anything user-facing, cursor pagination (Relay-style: edges/node/cursor + pageInfo.hasNextPage) is the standard. It's stable across mutations and aligns with the Apollo/Relay tooling you're already using.

Right. Let's make it a convention then: every paged GraphQL endpoint must use cursor pagination with the Relay connection spec. No more offset/limit in new schema. I'll add a CI lint rule next week to enforce it.

Good call. One nuance: when you migrate the existing offset endpoints, you'll want to expose both shapes for one minor version so consumers can switch over. The Relay spec also has well-defined `first`/`after`/`last`/`before` semantics — pick `first`+`after` as the canonical pair and reject `last`+`before` unless explicitly needed.

expected.note: The convention is 'use cursor pagination'. Either word 'cursor' or 'Relay' is accepted via the haystack match.
